May 2026 Engagement Survey · crewdite brief

Where We Are, What We Do Next.

A full-picture read on team health across departments, managers, tenure groups, and demographics — with a clear-eyed action plan for the weeks ahead.

Section 1 — The Numbers
What the data says, all up.

13 factors tracked across engagement, culture, leadership, career, and AI readiness. Scores are % favorable (agree or strongly agree).

⚠️ Participation note — worth a closer look
71% participation this cycle (68 of 96 employees) — lower than our typical range. Two likely contributors: employees currently on parental leave were included in the distribution, and Product & Marketplace had the lowest response rates of any department. Before drawing strong conclusions from team-level scores, it's worth asking Ruthie and Sarah what they're hearing — whether this reflects caution about psychological safety, apathy, or simply timing and logistics matters for how we read the results.

76%
The headline: 89% engagement is genuinely strong. The company's foundation — mission, safety, team quality — is holding. The gaps are in how clearly leadership is translating strategy into day-to-day meaning, and whether people see a career path. These are solvable problems, but they compound over time.
How We Stack Up — New Tech (100–200) Benchmarks
New Tech (100–200) comparison data is available for 3 of 13 factors. Top 10% data available for all 3. Cohort: New Tech companies (100–200 employees), January 2026.
Factor Propel New Tech (100–200) vs. Benchmark Top 10% vs. Top 10%
Feedback & Recognition 86% 82% +4pp ↑ 95% −9pp
My Manager 84% 86% −2pp ↓ 97% −13pp
Learning & Career Dev 76% 71% +5pp ↑ 86% −10pp
The benchmark story: On all 3 factors with New Tech (100–200) comparison data, Propel beats the benchmark on Feedback & Recognition (+4pp) and Learning & Career Dev (+5pp), but sits 2pp below on My Manager — driven by lower scores on consistent feedback quality. The Top 10% column shows where the ceiling is; closing those gaps is an 18-month project, not a quarter.
Section 1B — Historical Trends
How we've moved over 18 months.

Four survey periods tracked. New Tech (100–200) benchmark ≈ 73% overall — Propel crossed it in December 2025 and has stayed above it since.

Overall Engagement Trend vs. New Tech Benchmark
The arc: After 12 months of near-flat scores (69%→70%), engagement broke out dramatically in Dec 2025 (+20pp), then held steady at 89% in May 2026. Propel is now +16pp above the New Tech (100–200) benchmark.
A notable improvement: Learning & Career Development has made meaningful progress — up to 76% in May 2026 from 48% in December 2024, and now above the New Tech benchmark of 74%. This is worth acknowledging. The trajectory is moving in the right direction.
Factor-by-Factor Progression
Four survey periods. Overall New Tech (100–200) benchmark = 73%.
Factor Dec '24 Jun '25 Dec '25 May '26 Change
Overall Engagement 69% 70% 90% 89% +20pp
Mission / Pride 96% 93% 99% 99% +3pp
Leader Vision (communicates direction) 64% 63% 89% 82% +18pp
Confidence in Leaders 72% 72% 89% 79% +7pp
Psychological Safety — — 89% 88% ~flat
Decision-making Clarity — — 61% 69% +8pp
Career Opportunities / Learning & Career Development 48% 45% 62% 76% +28pp
Context: Why Several Scores Dipped from December 2025

Dec 2025 was a high-water mark built on exceptional circumstances. May 2026 reflects a harder baseline.

December 2025's 90% engagement wasn't a steady-state number — it came off the momentum of Propel navigating the government shutdown with remarkable grace, which was a defining win for the team. The months since have brought a different kind of test: the AI Native rollout, User Day, the departure of two Crew members, and the FIS Project — each creating real organizational weight.

The fact that engagement held at 89% through all of that is a sign of resilience, not stagnation. But several individual factors — particularly leader vision, decision-making clarity, and career development — show that people are processing uncertainty and looking for more signal about where things are heading.

The next 6 months are critical.

How Propel handles the VP of People transition, the Marketplace KR trajectory, AI adoption momentum, and career path clarity will determine whether engagement trends toward 95%+ or begins to erode. The mission foundation is strong. The opportunity is to match it with execution clarity and people investment that keeps the team feeling supported to do their best work.

Section 3 — Leadership Priorities
What leadership can own right now.

These are the highest-leverage moves available to department heads and the exec team — no programs, no budget, no People VP required. These are communication and behavior changes.

🎯 Priority 1 — Strategy Translation

"REI curation," "Innovation Eats First" — translate or retire them

Multiple employees wrote detailed comments explaining that they hear strategy language but can't translate it into what their team should do differently tomorrow. 82% favorable on "leaders have communicated a vision" sounds fine — but the neutral and disagree comments reveal real frustration.

"I'd rate this lower because there's a gap between our stated strategic direction and what I see in practice. Our Marketplace business hasn't changed fundamentally and I haven't seen meaningful work go into the information architecture of the app. There's been blue-sky thinking from design team, but not the research or experimentation that would move us toward a less overwhelming user experience."

Ask of leadership: At the next AHM and in a Slack post, each exec should take 5 minutes to answer: "What does [their strategic theme] mean in practice, what did we do last quarter to live it, and how will we know if we're doing it?" Concrete, not conceptual.
🎯 Priority 2 — Recognition for "Glue Work"

The 25% who don't feel recognized are doing invisible work

The comments are specific: coordination work, catching problems before they land, cultural labor (onboarding buddies, retreat logistics), and work whose outcomes are hard to attribute cleanly — none of it is getting named out loud.

"As a 'glue person,' a lot of my work is catching things. It was particularly tough to have some things just assumed they shook out well naturally, versus being the result of some real work and good judgment."

Ask of department heads: Add 2 minutes of explicit recognition to every team meeting for the next month. Name what the person did, not just that they did a "great job." This costs nothing and changes the felt experience fast.
🎯 Priority 3 — Decision-Making Ownership

69% clarity on who owns what — and the comments show the real cost

The most consistent operational complaint in the survey: work without a named owner defaults to whoever is closest. This creates invisible overload, shallow execution, and resentment. It's particularly acute in cross-functional work.

"The biggest friction point is unclear ownership of cross-functional work. Some work doesn't have a defined owner, so it defaults to whoever's closest. People end up taking on scope that should be a staffing or prioritization decision."

Ask of department heads: Publish a simple decision map for your team — what decisions live with you, what's delegated, what requires cross-functional alignment. It doesn't need to be a formal RACI. A Slack post or Notion page is enough.
🎯 Priority 4 — AI Adoption Needs an Owner

The early momentum has faded — and people feel it

Multiple comments reference that AI training felt strong in early spring and then "fell off once Alan left." There's no named leader, no accountability cadence, and norms around AI code review, quality, and maintenance remain unresolved — particularly for engineering.

"I thought we had a great start here in early spring that really fell off once Alan left. There's support and training if you are proactive about it, but given how much we are pushing this as the norm, there should be some more community building around AI-learning, along with accountability."

Ask of exec team: Name someone as AI adoption lead. It doesn't require a new role — it requires someone whose job it is to coordinate the community, track adoption, and publish norms. Monthly cadence, not weekly posts.
🎯 Priority 5 — Marketplace Cross-Functional Alignment

The Marketplace team sees a gap between what leaders say and what they do together

Multiple Marketplace employees wrote that they watch Product, Engineering, Marketing, and Marketplace operate without a shared plan — and that it directly blocks their ability to execute. This is a leadership visibility issue as much as a structural one. The team can see whether their leaders are aligned. Right now, they don't think they are.

Ask of Ari, Ram/Ruthie, Amar: Post a visible joint update — even a short Slack message — about how you're working together, what the shared roadmap looks like for next quarter, and where the cross-functional decision forums live. People are watching for signals that the leaders of these functions are in sync.

What's working.

The thread running through all of this: Propel employees believe in what they're building. The mission connection score (99%) is the foundation everything else rests on — and User Day at the retreat is a concrete example of how to keep reinforcing it. The learning & career development turnaround shows that when we invest in people's growth, they notice. These aren't abstract culture metrics — they're things we did.
Section 6 — Demographic Signals
Three patterns worth watching closely.

None of these are emergencies, but each has a compounding dynamic if ignored. Flagging now while they're still early signals.

🕐 Tenure Pattern

The 3–6 month cohort is struggling

Engagement drops noticeably at 3–6 months — classic "reality check" territory — then recovers. But a secondary dip at 4–6 years is worth watching.

The 3–6 month cohort also dips to 61% on Alignment and 67% on Leadership — far below company average. These folks are past onboarding but haven't yet found their footing. The 4–6 year dip is more subtle, but this is your institutional knowledge base. Protect it.
⚧ Gender Gaps

Men trail significantly on alignment & decision-making

Overall engagement is close (women: 90%, men: 88%), but meaningful gaps appear on strategic clarity and involvement. Women score lower on AI — worth watching as AI becomes more central to performance.

The 21-point gap on decision-making clarity is the largest gender gap in the dataset. Male employees feel significantly less clear on who owns what. Separately: one employee raised a concern about women being disproportionately "voluntold" for non-promotable work. Worth monitoring.
✨ Bright Demographic Signal

The mission connection is near-universal

99% overall, 100% for women, 97% for men, 100% across all tenure bands through 4+ years. This is Propel's foundation. Everything else in this brief assumes it holds — and right now, it does.

User Day at the retreat was mentioned in at least 12 separate comments as one of the most meaningful experiences at Propel this year. It moved the needle on mission connection. Schedule another one.

Section 8 — Action Plan
What happens next, and who owns it.

Sequenced by urgency and ownership. Items are specific, named, and sized to fit real calendar constraints.

⚡ Right Now (Weeks 1–2)
No People VP required
Exec team: address strategy translation in upcoming AHM segments and Lunch & Learns
Use the next few AHM segments and L&Ls to have each exec answer: "Here's what [REI curation / Innovation Eats First / Marketplace direction] means in practice, what we did last quarter, and how we'll know it's working." Concrete, not conceptual — and repeated in multiple formats so it lands.
Owner: CEO + Exec Team
Department heads: add explicit recognition to every team meeting for 4 weeks
2 minutes, named work, specific contribution. Especially: glue work, coordination, catching problems. Share what you're doing in #people-ops as a model for others.
Owner: All Department Heads
Ari / Ram / Amar: visible joint Marketplace alignment post
The Marketplace team is watching to see if their leaders are in sync. A short Slack post saying "here's how we're building Q3 together" goes a long way.
Owner: Ari, Ram, Amar
Schedule skip-level 1:1s with Design team
17% decision-making clarity requires a direct conversation with each team member about what they're missing, before it becomes an attrition problem.
Owner: Courtney + Exec
📅 Near-Term (Weeks 3–8)
Caroline + managers can own
Pulse check for 3–6 month cohort (6 people)
5-question check-in. Focus on: do you know what success looks like in your role, are you getting regular feedback, do you feel connected to company direction? Quick to run, high signal.
Owner: Caroline
Department heads: publish team decision maps
Simple Notion page or Slack post: what decisions live with the manager, what's delegated to ICs, what requires cross-functional alignment. Doesn't need to be a formal RACI.
Owner: Department Heads
Incorporate more user touchpoints into the employee experience
12+ employees cited User Day as one of the best Propel experiences this year. Look for ongoing ways to weave user stories, calls, and moments into the employee experience — not just one-off events.
Owner: Andie, Exec + Caroline
Name an AI adoption lead and address gaps in structured support
Momentum has faded since early spring. Name an owner who can identify where adoption is still lagging and provide structured support — function-specific tracks, peer learning, and clear norms — for those who need more than self-serve resources. Especially relevant for Gov, Benefits, and Brand/Marketing based on squad data.
Owner: Exec Decision → AI Lead
Continue to communicate transparently around VP of People Transition
Name what will be held, what will continue, and what the backfill timeline looks like. Silence will be filled with anxiety — especially for the 3–6 month cohort.
Owner: Caroline + CEO
🗓 Medium-Term (1–3 months)
Needs People VP backfill to own
Career ladder audit for uncovered roles
At least one employee has no documented career framework. Multiple others don't know what "next level" looks like. This requires a VP to own and champion credibly.
Owner: People VP (post-hire)
L&D budget review & benefits survey
The current L&D budget is mentioned repeatedly as insufficient — a formal review is needed. Separately, we'll survey employees on benefits priorities, with a focus on unlimited PTO utilization and what people actually want — not a unilateral decision. Employee input should drive what we change.
Owner: People VP (post-hire)
Compensation refresh — with focus on bonus structure
We walked through our comp framework a few months ago. This cycle, we'll do a refresh for anyone who missed it or needs a reminder — and more importantly, shift focus to what's still missing: a clear, credible bonus structure. Multiple comments call this out specifically. The framework exists; the variable comp story doesn't yet.
Owner: People VP + Finance
The thread that runs through everything: Propel's engagement is strong because the mission is strong. The risks are almost entirely about translation — translating strategy into work, translating recognition into daily behavior, translating vision into career paths. These are leadership communication challenges, not program design challenges. Most of the high-priority actions above require minutes per week, not budget or headcount.

Factor Heatmap — All Departments
All 13 factors across 8 departments with sufficient data. Color = % favorable.
≥90%   80–89%   70–79%   <70%   n/a
Factor Eng Mktplace Marketing Design Brokerage Data Sci Gov P&T
Every department clears the New Tech benchmark. The most remarkable turnaround is Government (+26pp since Dec 2024) and Engineering (+22pp). The factor heatmap shows the red concentrated in Marketplace (Leadership 67%, Learning & Career Development 58%, Alignment 64%) and Design (Leadership 50%, Alignment 50%, Learning & Career Development 67%) — which is why those are the focus areas in Section 4.
Participation caveat: Product (33%), Marketplace (63%), and Engineering (68%) had the lowest response rates. Marketplace and Engineering scores represent the majority of the company headcount, so low participation here is the most significant data quality issue to flag.
Opportunities — Marketplace & Design
Marketplace & Design have the most to gain.

Two teams are significantly below company averages across multiple factors. Small sample sizes mean individual scores can swing — but the pattern and the comments tell a consistent story. These are the highest-leverage improvement opportunities in the survey.

The questions with the biggest pull on overall engagement
These three questions have the strongest statistical correlation to overall engagement scores — moving them is where the leverage is.
Highest driver of engagement
75%

Team environment allows me to perform at my best

22% neutral, 3% unfavorable. With high impact on overall engagement, this is the single most important thing to move.

Highest driver of engagement
75%

I receive appropriate recognition for my work

19% neutral, 6% unfavorable. Comments reveal that "glue work" — coordination, catching things, cultural labor — consistently goes unacknowledged.

Strong driver · Watch
82%

Leaders have communicated a vision that motivates me

13% neutral, 5% unfavorable. The number looks fine but the comments tell a sharper story — specific strategic themes are landing as empty language.

🔴 Marketplace — 82% Engagement

The biggest team outside Engineering, and the one driving revenue. Multiple factors are significantly below company average.

FactorMarketplaceCompany AvgGap
Engagement82%89%▼ 7pp
Alignment & Involvement64%76%▼ 12pp
Leadership67%79%▼ 12pp
Learning & Career Development58%76%▼ 18pp
Career Opportunities42%69%▼ 27pp
Decision-Making Clarity50%69%▼ 19pp
Leader Vision58%82%▼ 24pp
Communication75%82%▼ 7pp
What's driving this?
  • Tension between revenue goal and user mission — "when the focus moves to what we get paid more for, I worry we become exploitative"
  • Marketplace doesn't feel like it has a seat at the product table — "doesn't seem to have a clear seat at the table when it comes to identifying and driving our revenue plans"
  • Tooling frustration — non-standardized ad platforms, manual reporting, no shared standards for AI adoption
  • 42% on career opportunities is the lowest of any department — this is a flight risk signal
Do We Have a Plan?

Not visibly. Comments suggest people are watching for one. Two immediate actions:

  1. Joint Marketplace leadership post — Ari, Ram/Ruthie, Amar on how they're building the roadmap together and what Q3 looks like
  2. Direct team conversation — the 42% career score needs a frank conversation about what growth looks like in Marketplace roles, even if formal ladders have to wait

"The lack of clear alignment between product, engineering, marketing, and marketplace on the roadmap, calendar, and core needs — there isn't as healthy a collaborative environment as desired. This leads to slower decision making processes and difficulty contributing at maximum. I'd like to see the leaders of those organizations providing more frequent and transparent communications on how we are collaborating."

— Marketplace employee, June 2026

🔴 Design — 80% Engagement

Small team (n=6), but a critical function. One number here is the most alarming in the entire dataset.

FactorDesignCompany AvgGap
Engagement80%89%▼ 9pp
Alignment & Involvement50%76%▼ 26pp
Leadership50%79%▼ 29pp
Decision-Making Clarity17%69%▼ 52pp
Career Opportunities50%69%▼ 19pp
Team Environment50%75%▼ 25pp
Leader Vision67%82%▼ 15pp
Values & Culture72%81%▼ 9pp
🚨 17% on Decision-Making Clarity

This is the single lowest score in the entire dataset. A 52-point gap from the company average. The Design team fundamentally doesn't know who owns what or how to escalate. This isn't a small problem — it's a structural breakdown that makes the team unable to function at its best.

What's happening?

Comments suggest design leadership hasn't been able to clearly articulate where the app is going. There's "blue sky thinking" without the research or experimentation to back it. The team knows what they're not getting — and it's a clear design vision with named ownership.

Immediate actions: (1) Executive skip-levels with Design team members in the next 2 weeks. (2) Design lead communicates a concrete 90-day direction — not a vision, a plan. (3) Decide explicitly: who owns design decisions on product roadmap items?
By Squad
Squad-level breakdown — May 2026.

10 squads, 57 respondents. Company overall: 89%. New Tech (100–200) benchmark: 74%. The story here is bifurcated: 8 squads at or above 84%, 2 Marketplace squads dragging the average — and the factor scores show exactly why.

The Marketplace split is the headline. Marketplace Biz (82%) and Marketplace EPD (83%) are the two lowest squads, and their factor scores converge on the same problems: Leadership 67%, Alignment & Involvement ~62%, Learning & Career Development and Values & Culture both ~72%. These aren't independent signals — they're the same team with the same root issues, showing up in both the business and product/eng sides.
